questionText: State the exact period of $y = \cot x$, and explain why it is different from the period of $y = \sec x$.

steps.explanation: Cotangent is cosine over sine (equivalently the reciprocal of tangent), so its repeating pattern comes from how $\cos x$ and $\sin x$ behave together.
Moving $180^\circ$ around the unit circle sends a point to the diametrically opposite point, which flips the sign of both cosine and sine. These are standard related-angle results.

steps.description: Form cot of the shifted angle
steps.explanation: Building the ratio for the shifted angle, both the numerator and the denominator pick up a minus sign.
The two minus signs cancel, so a $180^\circ$ shift leaves cotangent completely unchanged. This is why its period is only half that of sine and cosine.
For secant there is only one cosine, so the single minus sign survives and the value changes sign. Secant therefore does not repeat after $180^\circ$; it needs the full $360^\circ$.

steps.description: State the period in both units
steps.explanation: In radians $180^\circ$ is $\pi$, so the period of cotangent is $\pi$.
Cotangent repeats twice as often as secant because the paired sign-changes in $\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}$ cancel, whereas the lone cosine in $\sec x$ does not.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $y=\cot x$ has period $180^\circ$ (that is $\pi$ radians) — half the $360^\circ$ period of $\sec x$ — because the sign changes in $\dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}$ cancel after a $180^\circ$ shift, while the single cosine in $\sec x$ does not.
